edges.description: Neuronal imprinting limits compensatory expression from the paternal allele.
Effective neuronal UBE3A protein dosage falls below physiologic levels.
Lower UBE3A dosage reduces normal ubiquitination activity in neuronal systems.
Loss of ubiquitination control contributes to broad pathway-level signaling derangement.
Broad pathway dysregulation contributes to synaptic plasticity and remodeling defects.
Synaptic remodeling defects alter network excitability and transmission balance.
Synaptic remodeling dysfunction contributes to severe cognitive impairment.
Synaptic-network disruption contributes to profound expressive language deficits.
Network-level motor-circuit dysfunction contributes to persistent gait ataxia.
Synaptic remodeling failure across cortical circuits manifests as universal early developmental delay.
UBE3A-dependent neuronal/synaptic developmental defects contribute to acquired postnatal microcephaly.
Synaptic remodeling defects in motor circuits produce limb tremulousness.
Cortical and limbic synaptic-circuit dysfunction contributes to hypermotoric/hyperactive behavior.
Disrupted limbic-cortical synaptic networks contribute to the characteristic happy demeanor and unprovoked laughter.
Hypotonia and motor-circuit dysfunction secondary to synaptic remodeling defects predispose to progressive spinal curvature.
Physiologic instability at synaptic and membrane levels manifests as hyperexcitable cortical activity.
edges.target: Cortical network hyperexcitability with abnormal EEG background

edges.description: GABA-A receptor cluster hemizygosity exacerbates excitatory/inhibitory imbalance and epileptiform network activity.
edges.source: GABA-A receptor cluster hemizygosity in deletion subtype
edges.target: Cortical network hyperexcitability with abnormal EEG background

edges.description: OCA2 haploinsufficiency reduces ocular pigmentation in deletion-type AS.
Reduced melanin during ocular development contributes to refractive abnormality, with high astigmatism enriched in the deletion subgroup.
OCA2 haploinsufficiency contributes to high myopia restricted to deletion-type AS.
Hyperexcitable cortical networks drive recurrent seizure phenotypes.

edges.description: Persistent network instability contributes to long-term sleep dysregulation.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: High astigmatism (≥2.00 DC) was significantly more prevalent in deletion patients (54% vs 19% [P = 0.004]).

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000729 | Autistic behavior | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: UBE3A loss in autonomic and enteric neuronal populations contributes to pervasive gastrointestinal dysmotility manifesting as constipation and gastroesophageal reflux across the AS lifespan.
nodes.id: Autonomic and enteric nervous system dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of gastrointestinal symptoms in Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The majority of patients' medical records indicated at least one symptom of gastrointestinal dysfunction, with constipation and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) the most common.

nodes.description: Seizure control is frequently achieved with combination anti-seizure medication regimens, with a newer-agent-forward approach for tolerability.

evidence.reference_title: Seizure treatment in Angelman syndrome: A case series from the Angelman Syndrome Clinic at Massachusetts General Hospital.
evidence.snippet: Newer antiepileptic drugs such as levetiracetam, lamotrigine, and clobazam, and to a lesser extent topiramate, appeared to be as effective - if not more so - as valproic acid and clonazepam while offering more favorable side effect profiles.

evidence.reference_title: Pharmacotherapeutic management of seizures in patients with Angleman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Evidence for treating seizures in AS mainly derives from low-quality studies. Levetiracetam and clobazam are the most commonly used ASMs.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Treatment of manifestations: Anti-seizure medication for seizures.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of gastrointestinal symptoms in Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The majority of patients' medical records indicated at least one symptom of gastrointestinal dysfunction, with constipation and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) the most common.

nodes.description: Hyperexcitable cortical networks produce persistent epileptiform liability and abnormal electroencephalographic backgrounds.
nodes.id: Cortical network hyperexcitability with abnormal EEG background

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: etiology, clinical features, diagnosis, and management of symptoms.
evidence.snippet: Seizures, abnormal electroencephalography, microcephaly, and scoliosis are observed in >80% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal coherence and sleep composition in children with Angelman syndrome: a retrospective EEG study.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with AS display characteristic EEG patterns including high-amplitude rhythmic delta waves.

evidence.reference_title: Longitudinal EEG model detects antisense oligonucleotide treatment effect and increased UBE3A in Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Delta power measured in the scalp EEG is a reliable biomarker for Angelman syndrome but varies widely across individuals and throughout development, making detection of a treatment effect using single measurements challenging.

nodes.description: UBE3A-dependent ubiquitination signaling is reduced, with downstream dysregulation of protein-stability control.

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Loss of UBE3A affects multiple signalling pathways in the brain.

evidence.reference_title: The Ubiquitin E3 Ligase UBE3A Regulates GRIPAP1 and PACSIN1 Proteins Linked to the Endocytic Recycling of AMPA Receptors.
evidence.snippet: We provide evidence UBE3A polyubiquitinates PACSIN1 and GRIPAP1 to regulate protein turnover, with potential implications for impaired activity-dependent synaptic plasticity observed in AS.

nodes.description: Neuronal excitatory/inhibitory balance and electrophysiologic homeostasis are disrupted in cortical networks.

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: It has been consistently shown that mouse models of Angelman syndrome show marked changes in synaptic plasticity and excitatory/inhibitory balance.

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A controls axon initial segment in the cortical pyramidal neurons.
evidence.snippet: The axon initial segment (AIS) is a critical regulator of neuronal excitability and the initiation site of action potentials.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002307 | Drooling | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Multiple brain signaling pathways become dysregulated rather than a single isolated pathway defect.

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: given the multitude of signalling mechanisms that are derailed, it is unlikely that targeting a single pathway is going to be very effective.

nodes.description: In deletion-type Angelman syndrome, hemizygous loss of the contiguous GABRB3-GABRA5-GABRG3 GABA-A receptor subunit cluster compounds neuronal UBE3A loss and contributes to a more severe epilepsy and EEG phenotype than in non-deletion genotypes.
nodes.id: GABA-A receptor cluster hemizygosity in deletion subtype

evidence.reference_title: Electrophysiological Phenotype in Angelman Syndrome Differs Between Genotypes.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome (AS) is a severe neurodevelopmental disorder caused by either disruptions of the gene UBE3A or deletion of chromosome 15 at 15q11-q13, which encompasses UBE3A and several other genes, including GABRB3, GABRA5, GABRG3, encoding gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptor subunits (β3, α5, γ3).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Characterization of Epilepsy in Children With Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Significant differences exist in the clinical expression of epilepsy in AS according to the underlying genotype, with earlier age of onset and more severe epilepsy in individuals with AS due to a chromosome 15 deletion.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001251 | Ataxia | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome (AS) is characterized by severe developmental delay or intellectual disability, severe speech impairment, gait ataxia and/or tremulousness of the limbs

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of gastrointestinal symptoms in Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The majority of patients' medical records indicated at least one symptom of gastrointestinal dysfunction, with constipation and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) the most common.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: etiology, clinical features, diagnosis, and management of symptoms.
evidence.snippet: Clinical findings present in all patients include developmental delay, which becomes apparent by 6-12 months of age

nodes.description: Highly purified cannabidiol is being used for refractory epilepsy associated with 15q11.2-q13 deletion (Angelman syndrome) and duplication syndromes; real-world data show meaningful seizure reduction and adjunctive behavioral/sleep benefits with good tolerability.
nodes.id: Highly purified cannabidiol for refractory epilepsy

evidence.reference_title: Real-world effectiveness of highly purified cannabidiol in epilepsy associated with 15q11.2-q13.1 duplication and deletion syndromes: A multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: At last observation, mean seizure reduction was 55.7% (95% confidence interval 38.7-72.7), with 63.6% patients achieving ≥50% reduction, 40.9% achieving ≥75% reduction, and 18.2% achieving seizure freedom.

evidence.reference_title: Real-world effectiveness of highly purified cannabidiol in epilepsy associated with 15q11.2-q13.1 duplication and deletion syndromes: A multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: Tonic seizures in dup15q and myoclonic seizures in AS showed the most notable reductions.

evidence.reference_title: Real-world effectiveness of highly purified cannabidiol in epilepsy associated with 15q11.2-q13.1 duplication and deletion syndromes: A multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: CBD was well tolerated; no patient discontinued CBD due to side effects alone, and retention at last visit was 81.8%.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: etiology, clinical features, diagnosis, and management of symptoms.
evidence.snippet: Clinical findings present in all patients include developmental delay, which becomes apparent by 6-12 months of age, severely impaired expressive language, ataxic gait, tremulousness of limbs, and a typical behavioral profile, including a happy demeanor, hypermotoric behavior, and low attention span.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001010 | Hypopigmentation of the skin | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: UBE3A-related network pathology impairs synaptic organization and remodeling in neuronal circuits.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: review of clinical and molecular aspects.
evidence.snippet: Studies of mouse models of AS have implicated UBE3A in experience-dependent synaptic remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: Lack of UBE3A-Mediated Regulation of Synaptic SK2 Channels Contributes to Learning and Memory Impairment in the Female Mouse Model of Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Like in male AS mice, long-term potentiation (LTP) was significantly reduced while long-term depression (LTD) was enhanced at hippocampal CA3-CA1 synapses of female AS mice, as compared to female WT mice.

evidence.reference_title: Activity-dependent degradation of Kv4.2 contributes to synaptic plasticity and behavior in Angelman syndrome model mice.
evidence.snippet: Moreover, deficits in mEPSC frequency and spike-timing-dependent long-term potentiation, as well as certain behaviors including cognitive inflexibility found in AS mice, are rescued when bred with Kv4.2 conditional knockout mice.

evidence.reference_title: Epilepsy in patients with Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome (AS) is a neuro-behavioural, genetically determined condition, characterized by ataxic jerky movements, happy sociable disposition and unprovoked bouts of laughter in association with seizures, learning disabilities and language impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0008947 | Floppy infant | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: Severe iris hypopigmentation (grades 3-4) was unique to the deletion subgroup (33% vs. 0%; P = 0.013), linked to OCA2 codeletion.

nodes.description: Low-glycemic-index dietary intervention is used as an adjunctive non-pharmacologic seizure-management strategy, with mixed efficacy signals but good tolerability.

evidence.reference_title: Seizure treatment in Angelman syndrome: A case series from the Angelman Syndrome Clinic at Massachusetts General Hospital.
evidence.snippet: The low glycemic index treatment also provided effective seizure control with minimal side effects.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and tolerability of a low-glycemic-index ketogenic diet in Angelman syndrome: findings from the DIANE study.
evidence.snippet: INTERPRETATION: While the LGID was well-tolerated and showed trends toward neurocognitive and seizure improvements, results were not statistically significant.

nodes.description: Angelman syndrome is initiated by disruption of the maternally inherited UBE3A allele (deletion, pathogenic variant, UPD context, or imprinting defect).

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome is caused by mutations affecting the maternally inherited UBE3A gene, which encodes an E3-ubiquitin ligase that is critical for typical postnatal brain development.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: etiology, clinical features, diagnosis, and management of symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: High myopia (> -6.00 D) occurred exclusively in deletion patients (n = 2).

nodes.description: In neurons, the paternal UBE3A allele is silenced by imprinting, so maternal disruption leads to loss of effective neuronal UBE3A expression.

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: in neurons only the maternally inherited UBE3A gene is expressed.

nodes.description: Codeletion of OCA2 in the typical 15q11.2-q13 deletion produces partial oculocutaneous albinism features (iris hypopigmentation, severe astigmatism, and high myopia) restricted to the deletion subtype.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: The 15q11.2-q13 deletions confer a distinct oculoalbinism phenotype mediated by OCA2 haploinsufficiency, featuring high myopia, severe astigmatism, and iris hypopigmentation.

evidence.snippet: Physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy with an emphasis on nonverbal methods of communication, including augmentative communication aids (e.g., picture cards, communication boards) and signing.

evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome is caused by the loss of UBE3A protein.

evidence.reference_title: The Ubiquitin E3 Ligase UBE3A Regulates GRIPAP1 and PACSIN1 Proteins Linked to the Endocytic Recycling of AMPA Receptors.
evidence.snippet: AS arises due to the neuronal loss of UBE3A, an E3 ligase that regulates protein abundance through the addition of lysine 48 (K48)-linked polyubiquitin chains to proteins targeted for degradation by the ubiquitin proteasome system (UPS).

evidence.reference_title: Epilepsy in patients with Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Epilepsy, often severe and hard to control, is present in 85% of patients within the first three years of life, although less than 25% develop seizures during the first year.

evidence.snippet: Clinical findings present in all patients include developmental delay, which becomes apparent by 6-12 months of age, severely impaired expressive language

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome: review of clinical and molecular aspects.
evidence.snippet: The behavioral features of AS include a happy demeanor, easily provoked laughter, short attention span, hypermotoric behavior, mouthing of objects, sleep disturbance, and an affinity for water.

nodes.description: Speech therapy with nonverbal augmentative strategies is recommended due to severe expressive language deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy with an emphasis on nonverbal methods of communication, including augmentative communication aids (e.g., picture cards, communication boards) and signing.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: Ophthalmic manifestations in Angelman syndrome (AS)-particularly strabismus surgical outcomes and genotype-phenotype correlations-remain inadequately characterized due to limited cohort sizes in prior studies.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman syndrome (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000486 | Strabismus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Standardized strabismus surgery (extraocular-muscle surgery) achieves stable ocular alignment in Angelman syndrome regardless of molecular subtype, with high success rates and minimal need for reoperation.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic phenotype and strabismus surgery in Angelman syndrome: genotype-specific risks and uniform surgical efficacy.
evidence.snippet: Surgical success (exodeviation ≤8Δ/esodeviation ≤5Δ at 1 year) was comparable (70% vs 77%; P > 0.05), with minimal exodrift (deletion, -14.9Δ; nondeletion, -12.3Δ) and no reoperations.

nodes.description: Ongoing management of reflux, feeding, and constipation is recommended.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Routine management of gastroesophageal reflux, feeding difficulties, constipation, and strabismus.

nodes.description: Tonsillectomy is used in selected Angelman patients with sleep-disordered breathing or sialorrhea, and should be paired with structured postoperative respiratory, pain, and feeding monitoring.

evidence.reference_title: Outcomes After Tonsillectomy in Children With Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Twelve children with Angelman syndrome underwent tonsillectomy: 7 for sleep-disordered breathing, 4 for sialorrhea, and 1 for recurrent tonsillitis.

evidence.reference_title: Outcomes After Tonsillectomy in Children With Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Nine (75.0%) children experienced postoperative complications, most frequently pooling of secretions and oxygen desaturations.

evidence.reference_title: Angelman Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome (AS) is characterized by severe developmental delay or intellectual disability, severe speech impairment, gait ataxia and/or tremulousness of the limbs

evidence.reference_title: UBE3A reinstatement as a disease-modifying therapy for Angelman syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Angelman syndrome is caused by mutations affecting the maternally inherited UBE3A gene, which encodes an E3-ubiquitin ligase that is critical for typical postnatal brain development.
